channel.title: 今日のギャグフィード
channel.description: Google Gemini APIによって毎日自動生成される今日のギャグフィードです。

item.title: 今日のギャグ (2026-06-17) 405
item.description: 警察官が「止まれ！」って言っても止まらない果物ってなーんだ？ 梨（なし）

item.title: 今日のギャグ (2026-06-16) 1567
item.description:
考え中... ユーザーの要求は、日本語の面白いオヤジギャグを1つだけ、余計な説明なしで出力すること。
ギャグを作成する。 オヤジギャグの特徴： - ダジャレ - 寒い、と言われるがクスッとくるもの - 古典的、またはちょっとひねりがあるもの
いくつか候補を考える： 1. 「ねえ、このカレー、ルーが足りないんだ。…ルーだけにね！」 2. 「課長、今日は元気がないですね。…何かシャキッとしないと、サメになっちゃいますよ！」（鮫とシャキッをかけたが、ちょっと複雑か） 3. 「この魚、新鮮で『イカ』してるね！」 4. 「お父さん、もうお腹いっぱいだよ。…はい、これ以上食べると『腹イッパイ』になりますからね！」（腹いっぱいと、腹一杯をかけたが、普通すぎるか） 5. 「昨日、カフェでコーヒーを頼んだら、『カフェイン』とされたよ。」（カフェインと感動されたをかけたが、少し無理があるか） 6. 「お前、最近顔が疲れてるんじゃないか？…まさか、仕事で『しんどい』思いしてるの？」（しんどいとシンドイをかけたが、これも普通） 7. 「このケーキ、すごく甘いね。…まるで君の『甘い言葉』みたいだね！」（これはオヤジギャグというより口説き文句か） 8. 「電車の中でスマホをいじりすぎると、目が『つり革』になるぞ！」（つり革と疲れるをかけたが、無理がある） 9. 「ねぇ、お皿洗ってくれる？…『あらい』、助かるわー！」（あら、と洗いをかけた。これはシンプルで良い。） 10. 「このラーメン、美味しいね！…でも、ちょっと『麺』どうかな？」 （麺と面倒をかけた。これも定番で分かりやすい。）
上記の中から、シンプルでオヤジギャグらしいものを一つ選ぶ。
「ねぇ、お皿洗ってくれる？…『あらい』、助かるわー！」 これは分かりやすくて良い。
「このラーメン、美味しいね！…でも、ちょっと『麺』どうかな？」 これも良い。
どちらか一方。 「あらい、助かるわー」の方が、より会話調で、オヤジが言いそうな感じがする。
最終決定：「ねぇ、お皿洗ってくれる？…『あらい』、助かるわー！」
よし、これでいこう。

item.title: 今日のギャグ (2026-06-16) 552
item.description: カフェで「これ、コーヒー？」って聞いたら、「はい、こうひいてます」って言われたよ！

item.title: 今日のギャグ (2026-06-16) 609
item.description: 考えないでください、それは「カンガルー」です！

item.title: 今日のギャグ (2026-06-16) 459
item.description: 昨日、パン屋さんで「パンは焼き立てですか？」って聞いたら、店員さんに「いいえ、私の名前は佐藤です」って言われたんだ。

item.title: 今日のギャグ (2026-06-16) 372
item.description: はい、チーズ！僕のギャグはスベるけど、心は温かいんだから！

item.title: 今日のギャグ (2026-06-16) 815
item.description:
SCENE: 飲み会
「お父さん、また変なこと言ってる」
「え？ 俺、今まさに『新発見』してるんだがな。『今日の寿司ネタ、マグロじゃなくてアワビだった！』って」

item.title: 今日のギャグ (2026-06-15) 653
item.description:
SIGNS OF OLD AGE THOUGHTS: The user wants a single, interesting "Oyaji Gag" (dad joke) in Japanese. I need to come up with a pun or a play on words that is typical of an "oyaji gag". It should be concise and directly the joke itself, with no extra text.
Let's brainstorm some typical dad joke themes: - Food puns - Animal puns - Work puns - Everyday object puns
Consider a food pun. - "布団が吹っ飛んだ" (futon ga futtonda - the futon flew away) - Too common. - "これ、お水ですか？ いえ、たしかに水ですが、これはコップです。" (Is this water? Yes, it's water, but this is a cup.) - A bit too long, not a direct pun.
How about something related to weather or seasons? - "寒いギャグですね。" (Samui gyagu desu ne. - That's a cold joke, isn't it.) - Self-referential, but not *the* gag.
Let's try a common word and twist it. "パンはパンでも食べられないパンはなーんだ？" (What kind of bread can't be eaten?) -> "フライパン" (frying pan). - A bit too much like a children's riddle.
I need something that elicits a groan, which is the hallmark of an oyaji gag.
Think about a common phrase or situation.
What about a play on words with "ありがとう" (arigato - thank you)? "ありがとう" -> "蟻が10匹" (ari ga ju-hiki - 10 ants). This is a classic, but maybe too common.
Let's try a different approach. A common object or a situation at home. What about "洗濯物" (sentakumono - laundry)? "洗濯機が喋った！なんて言ったと思う？" (The washing machine talked! What do you think it said?) "せんたくしまーす！" (Sentaku shima-su! - I'm going to wash!) -> This is a bit weak.
How about something with numbers or days? "日曜日に日曜大工をしたら、もう月曜だい！" (Nichiyoubi ni Nichiyou daiku wo shitara, mou Getsuyou dai! - If I do DIY on Sunday, it's already Monday!) - "月曜だい" sounds like "月曜日" (Monday) and "もう月曜だ" (It's already Monday). This has potential.
Let's refine it. "日曜大工、頑張ったね。もう、月曜だい！" (Nichiyou daiku, ganbatta ne. Mou, Getsuyou dai!) The pun is "月曜だい" (Getsuyou dai) sounding like "月曜日" (Getsuyoubi - Monday) but also meaning "It's already Monday!" (もう月曜だ). The "だ" at the end can be "だい" (dai) for emphasis or casualness. This plays on the tiredness after weekend work. It's a bit relatable.
Let's check the criteria: - Single gag - Japanese - No extra text
"日曜大工、頑張ったね。もう、月曜だい！" This works. It's concise, has a pun, and fits the "oyaji gag" style.
One more idea: "これ、何の味のグミ？" (What flavor is this gummy?) "グミはグミでも、食べられないグミはなんだ？" (What kind of gummy can't you eat?) -> "ゴミ" (gomi - trash). This is similar to the bread one. Maybe the Sunday one is better as it's more of a situation.
Let's stick with the Sunday/Monday one. It's a bit more subtle than a direct homophone.
Final check of the Japanese: "日曜大工、頑張ったね。もう、月曜だい！" It's a simple sentence structure. "日曜大工" (Nichiyou daiku) = DIY (literally Sunday carpentry) "頑張ったね" (Ganbatta ne) = You worked hard, didn't you. "もう" (Mou) = Already "月曜だい！" (Getsuyou dai!) = It's Monday! / It's already Monday! (pun with "Getsuyoubi")
This feels right.

item.title: 今日のギャグ (2026-06-15) 581
item.description: 部長が居眠りしている部下を見て一言。「君、もう夢中かい？」

item.title: 今日のギャグ (2026-06-15) 1398
item.description: 傘を貸してって言われたんだけど、断ったんだ。だって、「傘」は「貸さん！」からね！

item.title: 今日のギャグ (2026-06-15) 978
item.description:
THOUGHTS: The user wants a single, interesting "Oyaji gag" (dad joke) in Japanese. The output should *only* be the text of the gag, with no extra explanation, intro, or greetings.
I need to come up with a Japanese dad joke. Criteria for a good dad joke: 1. **Puns/Wordplay:** Often plays on similar-sounding words or different meanings of the same word. 2. **Slightly groan-worthy:** Not necessarily hilarious, but clever enough to elicit a small groan or a smile. 3. **Relatively simple:** Easy to understand.
Let's brainstorm some ideas.
* **Food related:** * パン (pan - bread) -> パンダ (panda) -> 「パンはパンでも食べられないパンは何だ？」「フライパン！」 (What kind of pan can't you eat? A frying pan!) - This is a classic riddle/gag, might be too well known. * お茶 (ocha - tea) -> お家 (ouchi - house) -> 「お茶を飲んで、お茶でもしようか。」 (Let's drink tea, and then let's go home/house.) - A bit weak. * イカ (ika - squid) -> 「イカはイカしてもらう？」 (Can I get a squid, please?) - Doesn't quite land. * エビ (ebi - shrimp) -> 「エビフライはエビフライする前に、まず何をしますか？」「エビをむきます。」 (What do you do before you fry shrimp? You peel them.) - Not a pun.
* **Animal related:** * キリン (kirin - giraffe) -> 「キリンが一番長いのって何？」「首。」 (What's longest on a giraffe? Its neck.) - Not a pun. * ゾウ (zou - elephant) -> 「ゾウさんがいつも持っているものって何？」「花。」 (What does an elephant always have? A flower/nose.) - Hana (nose) is a bit too direct.
* **Daily life/Objects:** * 布団 (futon) -> 「布団が吹っ飛んだ！」 (The futon flew away!) - Classic, but maybe too simple. * 自転車 (jitensha - bicycle) -> 「自転車に乗ってて転んだら、何て言う？」「いてててて…。」 (If you fall off a bike, what do you say? Ow, ow, ow...) - No pun. * カバン (kaban - bag) -> 「カバンを貸してくれたのは、だれカバン？」 (Who lent me the bag? Someone!) - "誰か" (dareka - someone) vs "誰カバン". This has potential.
Let's try to refine the "カバン" one. "カバンを貸してくれたのは誰だ？" (Who lent me the bag?) "誰カバン？" (Dareka-ban? - pun on 誰か - dareka, meaning "someone")
Another one: * **Weather/Nature:** * 雨 (ame - rain) -> 飴 (ame - candy) -> 「雨が降ってきたら、傘をさすけど、飴が降ってきたら、どうする？」「食べる！」 (If it rains, you use an umbrella, but if it rains candy, what do you do? You eat it!) - Not really a pun gag. * 雷 (kaminari - thunder) -> 「雷が鳴っていると、どうなる？」「ピカッとして、ゴロゴロする！」 (What happens when there's thunder? It flashes and rumbles!) - No pun.
Let's go back to the classic structure: "XはXでもYできないXは何だ？" (What kind of X is it that is X, but cannot Y?) This is a very common dad joke format. "パンはパンでも食べられないパンはなーんだ？" "フライパン！" (Furai-pan - frying pan)
This is a good, solid, classic oyaji gag. It fulfills all criteria: 1. **Pun:** パン (pan - bread) vs フライパン (furai-pan - frying pan) 2. **Groan-worthy:** Definitely. 3. **Simple:** Yes. 4. **Japanese:** Yes. 5. **Output format:** Text only.
I think this is a good choice. Let's make sure the phrasing is natural. "パンはパンでも、食べられないパンはなーんだ？" (What kind of pan is it that is pan, but you can't eat it?) "フライパン！" (A frying pan!)
Yes, this works perfectly.

item.title: 今日のギャグ (2026-06-15) 1369
item.description: お肉と野菜、どっちが好き？って聞かれても、やっぱり『肉（にくい）』よね！

item.title: 今日のギャグ (2026-06-15) 1602
item.description:
THOUGHTS: The user wants a single, interesting "Oyaji gag" (dad joke) in Japanese. The output should *only* be the text of the gag, with no extra explanations, intros, or greetings.
I need to come up with a pun or simple wordplay typical of dad jokes. Let's try a few ideas:
1. **Food related:** * "ねぇ、このカレー、辛口だけど、もっとカラくならない？" (Hey, this curry is spicy, but can't it get *more* spicy? - pun on 辛口 / karakuchi and からく / karaku meaning 'spicy' or 'empty/hollow' - maybe too complex or not funny enough) * "お寿司が好きな人は、いつも『お寿司、好きだね！』って言われるよね。" (People who like sushi are always told 'You like sushi!' - pun on 'suki da ne' meaning 'you like it, don't you?' and 'suki' for liking. This is weak.) * "パン屋さんが焼きたてのパンを運んでる時、どこを見てると思う？ パンの道だよ！" (When a baker is carrying freshly baked bread, where do you think they're looking? The bread's path! - pun on 'pan no michi' (path of bread) and 'pan no mimi' (crust of bread) - this is a classic, but maybe not original enough.) * "コーヒー豆って、なんでいつも忙しいの？… 毎日マメに働いてるからね！" (Why are coffee beans always busy? ... Because they work diligently (mame ni) every day! - 'mame' means bean, and 'mame ni' means diligently/regularly. This is a good one. It's concise and uses a common Japanese pun.)
2. **Animal related:** * "ライオンが好きな野菜は何？ レタス (Let us)！" (What vegetable do lions like? Lettuce! - classic English one, doesn't work in Japanese.) * "ゾウさんがいつも持ってるものは何？ 忘れ物！" (What does an elephant always carry? Lost items! - this is a bit dark, maybe not 'interesting' and too common.) * "ネコが乗ってる車は何？…キャットカー！" (What car does a cat ride? A cat car! - too simple, not really a pun.)
3. **Everyday objects/situations:** * "パソコンって、なんでいつも疲れてるの？… 『キー』ボード叩きすぎてるからだよ！" (Why is a computer always tired? ... Because it's hitting the keyboard too much! - pun on 'key' (as in keys on a keyboard) and 'kii' (as in 'ki' from 'tsukareteru ki' - 'feeling tired'). A bit of a stretch.) * "警察官が好きな飲み物って何？… ホットコーヒー！(Hot coffee!)" (What drink do police officers like? Hot coffee! - pun on 'hotto' (relieved) and 'hotto cofee'. This is a common and decent one.)
Let's go with the coffee bean one or the police officer one. The coffee bean one is pretty standard and gets the point across well. The police officer one is also good.
"コーヒー豆って、なんでいつも忙しいの？… 毎日マメに働いてるからね！"
This one works. It's a classic style of dad joke in Japanese. The output should *only* be the gag text.
"コーヒー豆って、なんでいつも忙しいの？… 毎日マメに働いてるからね！"
Looks good.
